content: What are the main objectives of the ESMA Guidelines on sound remuneration policies under the AIFMD (ESMA/2013/232)?

The document you provided is a compliance table that shows which national competent authorities say they **comply or intend to comply** with the ESMA Guidelines on sound remuneration policies under the AIFMD (ESMA/2013/232). It lists the authorities, their status and occasional comments, but it does **not** include a description of the guidelines’ objectives.  

So, based on this document alone, I can’t tell you what the main objectives of the ESMA Guidelines are. If you need that information, you’ll have to refer to the actual text of ESMA/2013/232 or another source that outlines the policy goals.
What are the main objectives of the ESMA Guidelines on sound remuneration policies under the AIFMD (ESMA/2013/232)?

### Main objectives of the ESMA Guidelines (ESMA/2013/232)

The document you provided is a **compliance table** that shows which competent authorities comply with the ESMA Guidelines on sound remuneration policies under the AIFMD. It does **not** contain a description of the guidelines’ objectives.  

If you need the specific objectives, you’ll have to refer to the original ESMA Guidelines (ESMA/2013/232) or another source that outlines them.
What are the main objectives of the ESMA Guidelines on sound remuneration policies under the AIFMD (ESMA/2013/232)?

### Main objectives

The document you provided is a compliance table that lists which competent authorities **comply or intend to comply** with the ESMA Guidelines on sound remuneration policies under the AIFMD (ESMA/2013/232). It does **not** contain a description of the **objectives** of those guidelines. Therefore, based solely on this document, the main objectives cannot be identified.
Which EU member states’ competent authorities have indicated they comply or intend to comply with these guidelines, and where can the compliance status be verified?

### EU member states that say they **comply or intend to comply**

All of the EU countries listed in the table are marked “Yes” – meaning their national competent authority either already complies with, or plans to comply with, ESMA’s Guidelines on sound remuneration policies under the AIFMD – **except Denmark**, which explicitly states it will not comply.

So the compliant EU members are: Latvia, Luxembourg, France, Spain, Greece, Belgium, Slovakia, Czech Republic, Lithuania, the Netherlands, Austria, Portugal, Sweden, Hungary, Finland, Romania, Cyprus, Slovenia, Germany, Malta, Poland, Italy (both CONSOB and the Bank of Italy), Ireland, Estonia, Bulgaria, Croatia, and (historically) the United Kingdom up to 31 December 2020.

### Where to verify the status

The compliance status is recorded in ESMA’s “Guidelines compliance table” (ESMA/2016/675, dated 26 May 2025). You can check the latest version on the ESMA website (www.esma.europa.eu) or contact the individual competent authority directly – contact details are also provided on the ESMA site.

Why does the Danish Financial Supervisory Authority (FSA) choose not to comply with the guidelines, and what specific aspect of its national legislation creates the conflict?

The Danish Financial Supervisory Authority (FSA) says it won’t follow the ESMA remuneration guidelines because its national rules on **severance payments** clash with the ESMA framework. Denmark’s law creates a **threshold for “minor” severance payments** – essentially any payout that doesn’t exceed the amount of one or two years’ total remuneration (including pension). Those minor payments are exempt from the remuneration requirements that the AIFMD annex II (1)(k) and the ESMA guidelines impose.  

The ESMA guidelines, however, **don’t allow any such threshold** and expect all variable remuneration – including severance – to be subject to the same deferral and performance‑linkage rules (see paragraph 89). Because Denmark’s exemption means some severance payments fall outside the guideline’s scope, the FSA concludes it is “non‑compliant” with that part of the ESMA recommendations.
Why does the Danish Financial Supervisory Authority (FSA) choose not to comply with the guidelines, and what specific aspect of its national legislation creates the conflict?

### Why the Danish FSA does not comply

| Reason | Conflict with the ESMA Guidelines |
|--------|-----------------------------------|
| **Threshold for “minor” severance payments** | Danish law lets severance payments below a value equal to one‑ or two‑years’ total remuneration (including pension) be **exempt** from the AIFMD remuneration requirements. |
| **Guidelines require no such exemption** | ESMA’s Guidelines (para 89) state that *all* outstanding variable payments or long‑term incentive plans should be deferred and subject to the same rules; they do **not** allow a threshold‑based exemption. |
| **Result** | Because the Danish provisions create a carve‑out that the Guidelines expressly prohibit, the Danish FSA has declared it will not comply. |

In short, the Danish national legislation’s **threshold‑based exemption for minor severance payments** directly clashes with the ESMA guideline that mandates uniform treatment of all variable remuneration, leading the Danish FSA to opt out of compliance.
Why does the Danish Financial Supervisory Authority (FSA) choose not to comply with the guidelines, and what specific aspect of its national legislation creates the conflict?

The Danish Financial Supervisory Authority (FSA) does not comply because Denmark’s national rules on severance payments set a **threshold for “minor” severance payments**. Under Danish law, severance amounts that are equal to—or less than—a year or two years of total remuneration (including pension) can be exempted from the AIFMD remuneration requirements.  

ESMA’s Guidelines, however, **do not allow any such threshold**; they require that all severance payments be treated as variable remuneration and that any outstanding variable or long‑term incentive payments be deferred in line with paragraph 89 s. Since Denmark’s exemptions mean those minor severance payments fall outside the remuneration rules, the Danish FSA is considered “non‑compliant” with that specific part of the Guidelines.
How do the Danish exemptions for minor severance payments potentially affect alignment with AIFMD Annex II (1)(k) requirements on variable remuneration?

The Danish FSA’s rules let **“minor” severance payments** – those that don’t exceed roughly one or two years of total remuneration (including pension) – slip outside the **remuneration requirements**. The ESMA Guidelines (and AIFMD Annex II (1)(k)) expect severance payments to be tied to performance and, when they exceed that size, to be treated as **variable remuneration** subject to the same deferral and defection rules. Because Denmark’s national law creates a threshold that the Guidelines do not recognise, those smaller payments are **exempt** from the variable‑pay rules, meaning they are not forced to mirror the deferral schemes the Annex requires. This disconnect is why the Danish authority is labelled “non‑compliant” with the part of paragraph 89 that urges deferral of outstanding variable payments or long‑term incentive plans. In short, the Danish exemptions can pull the country away from the AIFMD’s variable‑remuneration standards.
How do the Danish exemptions for minor severance payments potentially affect alignment with AIFMD Annex II (1)(k) requirements on variable remuneration?

### Impact of the Danish exemptions on alignment with AIFMD Annex II (1)(k)

| Danish practice | AIFMD Annex II (1)(k) requirement | Potential effect on alignment |
|-----------------|-----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Minor severance payments are exempt** if they do not exceed a threshold (≈ 1‑2 years of total remuneration, including pension). | All variable remuneration must be **linked to performance** over time and must **not reward failure**. No thresholds are foreseen in the ESMA Guidelines. | Because the Danish rules allow certain severance payments to fall **outside** the variable‑remuneration regime, those payments are **not automatically tied to performance** as required by Annex II (1)(k). This creates a possible mis‑alignment, since the EU guidelines expect **no exemption thresholds** for variable remuneration. |
| Payments above the threshold are treated as variable remuneration and must meet the full set of requirements. | Same – they must be performance‑based and deferred where appropriate. | For larger payments, Denmark **does** apply the variable‑remuneration rules, so alignment is maintained for those amounts. |

**Bottom line:** The Danish exemption for minor severance payments means a slice of remuneration can escape the performance‑linkage and deferral rules of Annex II (1)(k), which is why the Danish Financial Supervisory Authority states it **does not** comply with the ESMA Guidelines.
How do the Danish exemptions for minor severance payments potentially affect alignment with AIFMD Annex II (1)(k) requirements on variable remuneration?

The Danish Financial Supervisory Authority (FSA) has a national rule that lets “minor” severance payments – those up to roughly one or two years of total remuneration (including pension) – be **exempted** from the AIFMD remuneration requirements.  

Because the ESMA Guidelines (and therefore Annex II (1)(k) of the AIFMD) do **not** allow any threshold, those exempted payments are not treated as “variable remuneration” and the related deferral and performance‑linkage rules do not apply. This creates a mismatch: the Danish approach → severance below the threshold is outside the variable‑remuneration regime, whereas Annex II (1)(k) expects **all** variable remuneration (including severance that exceeds the threshold) to be linked to performance and, where appropriate, deferred. Consequently, Denmark is considered “non‑compliant” with the part of the Guidelines that advises deferring outstanding variable payments, because its exemptions sideline those payments from the AIFMD‑mandated controls.

In what ways might the implementation of the guidelines by compliant authorities, such as the Hungarian Central Bank’s MNB recommendations 4/2018, influence remuneration practices of Alternative Investment Fund Managers (AIFMs) in those jurisdictions?

### How the Hungarian implementation could shape AIFM pay

Because the Central Bank of Hungary has **adopted the MNB recommendations 4/2018**, which expressly **implement the ESMA sound‑remuneration guidelines**, AIFMs operating in Hungary are required to bring their pay structures into line with those EU‑wide rules. In practice this means that any variable or incentive‑based compensation must satisfy the same conditions set out in the guidelines – for example, linking it to performance, applying appropriate deferral periods and avoiding rewards for failure.  

The document doesn’t detail the exact mechanisms of the guidelines, so we can only say that Hungarian AIFMs will need to **adjust their remuneration policies** to meet the standards that the MNB recommendations have embedded, ensuring full compliance with the ESMA framework.

In what ways might the implementation of the guidelines by compliant authorities, such as the Hungarian Central Bank’s MNB recommendations 4/2018, influence remuneration practices of Alternative Investment Fund Managers (AIFMs) in those jurisdictions?

### Influence of Guideline Implementation by Compliant Authorities  

| Authority | Implementation Note | Likely Effect on AIFM remuneration |
|-----------|----------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Hungary – Central Bank of Hungary (MNB)** | “MNB recommendations 4/2018 (l.16) implemented the Guidelines.” | AIFMs operating in Hungary must apply the ESMA sound‑remuneration policies as incorporated in the MNB 4/2018 recommendations. This means their remuneration structures (fixed pay, variable pay, severance, long‑term incentives, deferrals, etc.) must conform to the same standards that other compliant EU jurisdictions follow. |

**Key point:** The document only states that Hungary has *implemented* the ESMA guidelines through the MNB recommendations. It does not provide further detail on the specific remuneration changes or mechanisms that result from this implementation. Consequently, we can only infer that AIFMs in Hungary are required to align their remuneration practices with the ESMA sound‑remuneration framework.

In what ways might the implementation of the guidelines by compliant authorities, such as the Hungarian Central Bank’s MNB recommendations 4/2018, influence remuneration practices of Alternative Investment Fund Managers (AIFMs) in those jurisdictions?

The table shows that the Central Bank of Hungary — through its MNB Recommendations 4/2018 (l.16) — has **implemented the ESMA Guidelines on sound remuneration policies**. This means that, in Hungary, Alternative Investment Fund Managers are required to apply the same remuneration standards set out in the ESMA Guidelines (e.g., rules on variable pay, deferral, and performance‑related elements).  

However, the document does **not provide any further detail** about how those recommendations change specific pay structures, thresholds, or reporting requirements for AIFMs. It only confirms that Hungary is a compliant jurisdiction. Consequently, the precise influence on remuneration practices cannot be described from the information given.

Considering the varying compliance statuses across member states, what challenges could arise for cross‑border AIFMs in ensuring consistent remuneration policies, and how might they address these challenges?

Cross‑border AIFMs will run into the fact that almost every EU competent authority says it **complies** with ESMA’s remuneration guidelines, but Denmark **does not** – it keeps a threshold for “minor” severance payments that the guidelines expressly forbid. That creates a split: the same remuneration scheme could be acceptable in, say, France or Germany and then run afoul of Danish rules because part of the severance is treated as exempt rather than as variable pay.

Because the guidelines require a uniform approach to variable remuneration, AIFMs have to reconcile the stricter EU‑wide expectations with the Danish exemption. The practical way to do that is to design remuneration policies that meet the **most demanding** interpretation – i.e., treat all severance payments above the one‑ or two‑year‑salary level as variable remuneration and apply the full set of deferral and claw‑back rules. Where a national authority (like Denmark) offers a different treatment, the AIFM can either seek a waiver from the local regulator or adjust its contracts locally while keeping the core policy aligned with the EU standard. Keeping an eye on each authority’s compliance status (as the table shows) and staying in touch with them for any updates is essential to avoid surprises.

Considering the varying compliance statuses across member states, what challenges could arise for cross‑border AIFMs in ensuring consistent remuneration policies, and how might they address these challenges?

### Challenges for cross‑border AIFMs  

| Issue | Why it matters (based on the table) |
|-------|--------------------------------------|
| **Different national compliance** | Most EU/EEA states **comply** with ESMA’s remuneration guidelines, but **Denmark** (Danish FSA) **does not** and has no intention to do so. |
| **Divergent treatment of severance payments** | The Danish rules allow a threshold for “minor” severance payments, whereas the ESMA Guidelines **do not foresee any threshold**. This creates a mismatch in what is considered variable remuneration. |
| **Need to monitor authority positions** | Article 16(3) of Regulation (EU) No 1095/2010 requires authorities to report compliance or reasons for non‑compliance, and ESMA may publish those reasons. AIFMs must stay updated on each competent authority’s stance. |

### How AIFMs can address them  

- **Adopt the most restrictive approach** – design remuneration policies that satisfy the ESMA Guidelines (no severance‑payment thresholds) to remain compliant in all jurisdictions, including Denmark.  
- **Track authority updates** – regularly check the compliance table and contact the relevant competent authority (contact details are on the ESMA website) for any changes.  
- **Document justification** – keep a clear record of how the policy aligns with the Guidelines, which can be shown to national supervisors that require compliance evidence.  

By aligning with the ESMA baseline and staying informed of each authority’s position, cross‑border AIFMs can mitigate the inconsistency risk.

Considering the varying compliance statuses across member states, what challenges could arise for cross‑border AIFMs in ensuring consistent remuneration policies, and how might they address these challenges?

### Challenges for cross‑border AIFMs  

- **Different national stances** – Most competent authorities (e.g., Latvia, Luxembourg, France, Spain, etc.) state they **comply** with the ESMA Guidelines, but Denmark explicitly **does not** and cites its own severance‑payment thresholds as the reason. This creates a patchwork where the same remuneration rule may be applied differently.  

- **Policy‑gap risk** – In a jurisdiction that does not follow the Guidelines (Denmark), AIFMs could face “non‑compliant” treatment for variable or severance payments that are considered compliant elsewhere.  

- **Regulatory tracking** – The document notes that authorities must inform ESMA of any change in compliance status, and that the UK’s status is retained only for historical reference. AIFMs need to stay up‑to‑date on each authority’s position.  

### How they might address the challenges  

- **Adopt the ESMA Guidelines as a baseline** – Even in non‑compliant jurisdictions, apply the full ESMA remuneration requirements voluntarily to avoid gaps.  

- **Monitor national notices** – Regularly check the latest ESMA compliance table (or contact the relevant authority) to spot any status changes.  

- **Align with the strictest rules** – Design remuneration policies that satisfy the most demanding national interpretation (e.g., Denmark’s severance‑payment thresholds) so the same policy works everywhere.  

These steps help cross‑border AIFMs maintain consistent remuneration practices despite the uneven compliance landscape.
What is the purpose of ESMA’s publication of aggregated data on AIF exposures to commercial real estate, and how does it relate to the ESRB Recommendation E?

ESMA’s release of aggregated data on Alternative Investment Fund (AIF) exposures to commercial real‑estate (CRE) is meant to give a clear picture of how much capital EU‑supervised funds have invested in the sector. The move directly follows **Recommendation E** of the ESRB’s “Closing real‑estate data gaps” recommendation, which specifically urges ESMA to publish such aggregated exposure figures for the entities it oversees. By pulling the numbers from the AIFMD database and presenting the NAV, geographical split and total CRE value, ESMA is essentially fulfilling the ESRB’s call for greater transparency and better data coverage on real‑estate holdings across the EU.
What is the purpose of ESMA’s publication of aggregated data on AIF exposures to commercial real estate, and how does it relate to the ESRB Recommendation E?

### Purpose of the ESMA publication  

- To provide **aggregated data on the exposures of AIFs** (alternative investment funds) to commercial real‑estate (CRE) markets in the EU.  
- The data are drawn from the **AIFMD database** and cover the number of CRE‑focused AIFs, their NAV, geographical split of investments and the total value of CRE holdings as of 31 December 2024.  
- By publishing these figures, ESMA aims to **fill real‑estate data gaps** and give supervisors, policymakers and market participants a clearer view of CRE risk concentration within the alternative‑investment sector.

### Relation to ESRB Recommendation E  

- **Recommendation E** of the ESRB’s “Closing real‑estate data gaps” (ESRB/2016/14) specifically **asks ESMA to publish aggregated CRE exposure data** for entities it supervises.  
- ESMA’s release is a direct implementation of that recommendation, fulfilling the ESRB’s call for better transparency and data availability on CRE exposures across EU‑wide AIFs.
What is the purpose of ESMA’s publication of aggregated data on AIF exposures to commercial real estate, and how does it relate to the ESRB Recommendation E?

### Purpose and Link to ESRB Recommendation E  

ESMA is publishing aggregated data on the commercial‑real‑estate (CRE) exposures of alternative investment funds (AIFs) because **Recommendation E of the ESRB’s “closing real‑estate data gaps” guidance specifically asks ESMA to make such aggregated information publicly available**.  

The publication therefore serves to:

- Deliver EU‑wide, yearly figures on the number of CRE‑focused AIFs, their NAV, geographical split of investments and the total value of the underlying real‑estate assets.  
- Fill the data gap identified by the European Systemic Risk Board (ESRB), enabling better monitoring of systemic risk in the CRE market.  

In short, ESMA’s data release directly implements ESRB Recommendation E by providing the required aggregated exposure statistics for entities it supervises.
How many alternative investment funds (AIFs) pursuing a commercial‑real‑estate strategy were reported at the end of 2024, and what is the total net asset value (NAV) they manage?

At the end of 2024, **1,931 alternative investment funds** that are marketed and/or managed by authorised EU AIFMs were reported as pursuing a commercial‑real‑estate (CRE) strategy. Those funds together hold a **net asset value of about €531 billion**. (The broader count of all AIFs with a CRE focus, including those outside the EU‑passport regime, was 2,213, but the NAV figure quoted relates specifically to the 1,931 EU‑authorised funds.)
How many alternative investment funds (AIFs) pursuing a commercial‑real‑estate strategy were reported at the end of 2024, and what is the total net asset value (NAV) they manage?

### AIFs with a Commercial‑Real‑Estate (CRE) Strategy – End 2024

| Metric | Figure |
|--------|--------|
| Number of AIFs (marketed/​managed by authorised EU AIFMs) | **1,931** |
| Total Net Asset Value (NAV) managed by these AIFs | **€531 billion** |

*Note:* The broader universe includes 2,213 AIFs pursuing a CRE strategy, but the published data only covers the 1,931 funds that are marketed and/or managed by authorised EU AIFMs.
How many alternative investment funds (AIFs) pursuing a commercial‑real‑estate strategy were reported at the end of 2024, and what is the total net asset value (NAV) they manage?

### Answer
- **Number of AIFs pursuing a commercial‑real‑estate (CRE) strategy (end‑2024):** 1931 AIFs that are marketed and/or managed by authorised EU AIFMs.  
- **Total Net Asset Value (NAV) they manage:** EUR 531 billion.  

*Note:* The broader pool of CRE‑focused AIFs totals 2,213, but only the 1,931 AIFs under ESMA’s supervisory scope are included in the reported NAV figure.
What proportion of the reported CRE investments is located in the European Economic Area (EEA) versus outside the EEA, and how is this geographical breakdown calculated?

The ESMA data show that **about 83 % of the reported commercial‑real‑estate (CRE) investments are located in the European Economic Area (EEA), while roughly 17 % are outside the EEA**.

This geographical split is derived **by taking the total net asset value (NAV) of the CRE AIFs and allocating it according to the domicile of each underlying investment**. In other words, the share of NAV tied to assets domiciled in the EEA versus those domiciled elsewhere is expressed as a percentage of the overall NAV. The calculation is based on the information reported by AIFs that are marketed and/or managed by authorised EU AIFMs and that pursue a primary CRE strategy.
What proportion of the reported CRE investments is located in the European Economic Area (EEA) versus outside the EEA, and how is this geographical breakdown calculated?

### Geographical breakdown of CRE investments (as of 31 Dec 2024)

| Region | Share of reported CRE investments |
|--------|-----------------------------------|
| **European Economic Area (EEA)** | **83 %** |
| **Outside the EEA (Non‑EEA)**   | **17 %** |

### How the breakdown is calculated  

- The percentages are derived **as a share of the total Net Asset Value (NAV)** of the AIFs.  
- ESMA uses the **domicile of each investment** to allocate it to either the EEA or non‑EEA bucket.  
- The data come from **AIFs marketed and/or managed by authorised EU AIFMs** that pursue a primary commercial‑real‑estate strategy, as reported in the AIFMD database.  

So, 83 % of the NAV‑based CRE exposures are within the EEA, while the remaining 17 % are located outside the EEA.
What proportion of the reported CRE investments is located in the European Economic Area (EEA) versus outside the EEA, and how is this geographical breakdown calculated?

### Geographical breakdown of CRE investments (as of 31 Dec 2024)

| Region | Share of reported CRE investments |
|--------|-----------------------------------|
| **EEA** | **83 %** |
| **Non‑EEA** | **17 %** |

**How the breakdown is calculated**

- The percentages are derived from the **total Net Asset Value (NAV)** of the AIFs that pursue a commercial‑real‑estate (CRE) strategy.  
- ESMA calculates the share of each region by taking the **NAV allocated to assets domiciled in that region** and expressing it as a proportion of the overall NAV.  
- The data come exclusively from AIFs **marketed and/or managed by authorised EU AIFMs** that report under the AIFMD framework.  

So, 83 % of the reported CRE NAV is invested within the European Economic Area, while 17 % is located outside the EEA, based on NAV‑weighted exposure reported by the supervised AIFs.
Which types of funds or entities are excluded from the data set (e.g., sub‑threshold AIFMs, feeder AIFs), and what impact might these exclusions have on the completeness of the published figures?

The data set **does not include** AIFs that are managed by *sub‑threshold AIFMs*—those registered only at the national level and without an AIFMD passport. It also leaves out *feeder AIFs* to avoid counting the same assets twice. In addition, any commercial‑real‑estate exposure from entities that are not reporting under the AIFMD framework (for example, funds created under national laws) is omitted.

Because these groups are excluded, the published numbers capture only the AIFs marketed or managed by authorised EU AIFMs that report through the AIFMD passport. That means the figures – 1,931 AIFs, €531 bn NAV and €444 bn of CRE assets – represent about **9.8 % of the total CRE fund market** in terms of NAV. The omission of sub‑threshold managers, feeders and non‑AIFMD reporters likely understates the overall size and geographical spread of CRE exposure in the EU, so the published totals should be viewed as a partial, not exhaustive, picture.
Which types of funds or entities are excluded from the data set (e.g., sub‑threshold AIFMs, feeder AIFs), and what impact might these exclusions have on the completeness of the published figures?

### Funds / entities excluded from the data set

| Excluded type | Reason for exclusion (as stated in the document) |
|---------------|---------------------------------------------------|
| **Sub‑threshold AIFMs** (registered nationally, no AIFMD passport) | Not included in the table; they do not report under the AIFMD passport regime. |
| **Feeder AIFs** | Excluded to avoid any duplication of assets. |
| **Entities not reporting under AIFMD** (e.g., some national‑law structures) | Their commercial‑real‑estate exposures are not captured. |

### Impact on completeness of the published figures

- The NAV and CRE investment totals (EUR 531 bn and EUR 444 bn) represent only AIFs marketed/managed by authorised EU AIFMs, i.e., about **9.8 % of the total CRE funds** by NAV.  
- Excluding sub‑threshold AIFMs, feeder AIFs, and non‑AIFMD reporters means a portion of the CRE market is omitted, so the published numbers **do not reflect the full EU CRE exposure**.  
- Consequently, the data provide a **partial view** of the sector, useful for aggregated analysis but not exhaustive.
Which types of funds or entities are excluded from the data set (e.g., sub‑threshold AIFMs, feeder AIFs), and what impact might these exclusions have on the completeness of the published figures?

### Exclusions from the ESMA data set  

- **Sub‑threshold AIFMs** – AIFs managed by AIFMs that are below the AIFMD threshold and are only registered at national level (no EU passport).  
- **Feeder AIFs** – Excluded to avoid double‑counting assets that are already captured in the underlying “master” AIFs.  
- **Entities not reporting under AIFMD** – Includes some funds created under national laws that do not fall within the AIFMD reporting framework.  

### Impact on completeness  

- Because these groups are omitted, the published figures (1931 CRE‑focused AIFs, €531 bn NAV, €444 bn CRE value) do **not capture the full universe of CRE exposure** in the EU.  
- The total market size is likely **under‑stated**, as sub‑threshold managers, feeder structures, and non‑AIFMD entities can hold additional CRE assets that are not reflected in the aggregated numbers.  

In short, the data give a solid view of AIFs under ESMA supervision, but they do not represent the entire CRE fund landscape.
How is the “total value of CRE investments” defined in the report, and what sources of valuation are used when financial accounts are unavailable?

The report defines the **total value of CRE investments** as the value of the real‑estate assets that are physically held by the commercial‑real‑estate funds. When an AIF’s latest financial statements are available, that reported figure is used. If those accounts aren’t available, ESMA falls back to a **fair‑value** estimate of the property holdings. In short, it’s the on‑record or, where missing, the fair‑value appraisal of the physical CRE assets.
How is the “total value of CRE investments” defined in the report, and what sources of valuation are used when financial accounts are unavailable?

### Definition of “total value of CRE investments”

| Aspect | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| **What it measures** | The value of real estate **physically held** by the commercial‑real‑estate (CRE) funds. |
| **Primary source** | The **most recent financial accounts** of the AIF. |
| **Fallback source** | If the financial accounts are not available, the value is taken **at fair value**. |

In short, the total value is the reported market‑or‑fair‑value of the physical CRE assets, using the latest audited accounts wherever possible, and fair‑value estimates only when those accounts are missing.
How is the “total value of CRE investments” defined in the report, and what sources of valuation are used when financial accounts are unavailable?

### Definition of “total value of CRE investments”

The report defines the **total value of CRE investments** as **the value of the real‑estate assets physically held by the commercial‑real‑estate (CRE) funds**.  

### Valuation sources

- **Primary source:** The value reported in the AIF’s most recent financial accounts.  
- **Fallback source:** If the latest financial accounts are not available, the value is taken **at fair value**.

So, the total CRE investment figure is built from the latest audited accounts where possible, otherwise from a fair‑value estimate.
In what ways could regulators, investors, or policymakers use the disclosed CRE exposure data, and what limitations should they consider when interpreting the figures?

The CRE exposure figures give regulators a snapshot of how much capital authorised EU AIFMs are putting into commercial real‑estate, letting them track the sector’s size (EUR 531 bn NAV, EUR 444 bn of physical assets) and where the money is geographically split (83 % EEA, 17 % non‑EEA). Investors can use the same numbers to gauge the overall market depth and the relative weight of EU‑based funds in the CRE space. Policymakers can draw on the data to understand the exposure of the alternative‑investment industry to real‑estate assets and to shape any needed oversight or support measures.

But the numbers come with clear limits. They only cover AIFs marketed or managed by authorised EU AIFMs, exclude sub‑threshold AIFMs, feeder funds and any CRE exposure from entities that don’t report under AIFMD. The snapshot is as of 31 December 2024 and reflects only funds pursuing a pure CRE strategy, so it may miss other relevant holdings. In short, the data is useful for a high‑level view, but it doesn’t capture the full universe of CRE exposure in the EU.
In what ways could regulators, investors, or policymakers use the disclosed CRE exposure data, and what limitations should they consider when interpreting the figures?

### How the data can be used  

| User | Potential use (based on disclosed data) |
|------|------------------------------------------|
| **Regulators** | Track the aggregate size of CRE‑focused AIFs (NAV €531 bn) and their market share (9.8 % of total CRE funds). Monitor the EU vs. non‑EEA exposure split (83 % / 17 %). |
| **Investors** | Benchmark the scale of the CRE AIF market (1931 funds, €444 bn of physical CRE value). Compare geographical exposure patterns to assess diversification opportunities. |
| **Policymakers** | Gauge the overall exposure of EU‑authorized AIFMs to commercial real estate and identify the proportion of assets located outside the EEA, informing any cross‑border or macro‑prudential considerations. |

### Limitations to keep in mind  

- Only AIFs **marketed and/or managed by authorised EU AIFMs** are included; sub‑threshold AIFMs and feeder AIFs are excluded.  
- Entities **not reporting under AIFMD** (e.g., some nationally‑created funds) are omitted, potentially understating total CRE exposure.  
- Data reflect **AIFs pursuing solely a CRE strategy** as of 31 Dec 2024; mixed‑strategy funds are not captured.  
- Valuation relies on the **most recent financial accounts or fair value** where accounts are unavailable, which may differ across funds.  

These points should be considered when interpreting the figures.
In what ways could regulators, investors, or policymakers use the disclosed CRE exposure data, and what limitations should they consider when interpreting the figures?

### How the data can be used  

- **Regulators** can monitor the scale of CRE exposure in the EU‑AIF market (1931 funds, €531 bn NAV) and track where the assets are located (83 % EEA, 17 % non‑EEA). This helps assess concentration risks and the effectiveness of the ESRB recommendation.  
- **Investors** get a snapshot of the overall size of the CRE pool (€444 bn of physical/fair‑value assets) and can compare the share of EU‑authorized AIFMs (9.8 % of total CRE funds by NAV) with other market segments.  
- **Policymakers** can use the geographical breakdown and total value to gauge the importance of CRE for the EU economy and to design macro‑prudential or housing‑policy measures.

### Limitations to keep in mind  

- The figures only cover AIFs **marketed or managed by authorised EU AIFMs**; sub‑threshold AIFMs, feeder AIFs and entities not reporting under AIFMD are excluded.  
- Only funds pursuing **solely a CRE strategy** are counted, so mixed‑strategy funds are left out.  
- The data reflect the **end‑2024 reporting point** and rely on the most recent financial accounts or, if unavailable, fair‑value estimates.  
- Consequently, the disclosed numbers do **not capture all CRE exposures** in the EU, especially those from national‑law structures or non‑reporting entities.
What are the primary objectives of ESMA’s discussion paper on the integrated collection of funds’ data and how do they relate to existing EU reporting requirements?

**What the paper is trying to achieve**

ESMA’s discussion paper is all about getting a clearer, more efficient view of the data that fund managers have to send to supervisors. Its main aims are to:

1. **Map out the current reporting landscape** – the paper first takes stock of every supervisory and statistical reporting obligation that AIFMs, UCITS managers and MMF managers face at both EU and national level.  

2. **Identify duplication and gaps** – by spotting where templates overlap, where data semantics differ, and where important data points are missing, ESMA wants to understand how the existing framework is fragmented and where the reporting burden can be reduced.  

3. **Lay the groundwork for an integrated “report‑once” system** – the ultimate goal is to propose recommendations that will let fund managers submit data a single time, using a common data dictionary, consistent identifiers (LEI, ISIN, CFI, etc.) and harmonised formats (ISO 20022‑XML, XBRL or JSON).  

4. **Prioritise high‑value data** – the paper stresses that any new template should focus on data that really supports supervisory, macro‑prudential and statistical analysis, while trimming out redundant fields.  

**How this ties into today’s EU rules**

All of these objectives sit squarely on top of the reporting obligations already set out in the **AIFMD, UCITS Directive and MMFR** (the EU‑level supervisory frameworks) and the **ECB statistical reporting regime**. Those laws already require periodic, often quarterly or monthly, submissions on fund composition, risk exposures, leverage, liquidity, etc. What the paper does is not to add new data, but to **harmonise and streamline the existing requirements** so that the same information can satisfy both supervisory and statistical needs, reduce the dozens of templates currently in use, and make the “report‑once” principle work across the whole asset‑management sector. In short, the paper’s objectives are a direct response to the fragmentation and overlap that the current EU reporting framework creates.
What are the primary objectives of ESMA’s discussion paper on the integrated collection of funds’ data and how do they relate to existing EU reporting requirements?

### Primary objectives of the ESMA discussion paper  

| # | Objective | How it connects to the current EU reporting regime |
|---|-----------|---------------------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Map the existing supervisory and statistical reporting landscape** (AIFMD, UCITS, MMFR, ECB statistical rules, etc.) | Uses the reporting templates already mandated by the **AIFMD** and **UCITS** directives and the **MMFR** to create a baseline “stock‑take”. |
| 2 | **Identify and reduce duplication & inconsistencies** (over‑laps, gaps, semantic differences) | Directly targets the **fragmentation** highlighted by the 2020 EU‑supervisory reporting fitness‑check and the multiple national templates that coexist with the EU‑level ones. |
| 3 | **Promote data standardisation and a common data dictionary** (LEI, ISIN, CFI, ISO 20022, etc.) | Aligns with the identifiers already required in **AIFMD**, **UCITS**, **EMIR**, **SFTR** and **MiFID/MiFIR**, and builds on the EU‑wide data‑dictionary initiatives listed in Annex 11. |
| 4 | **Advance the “report‑once, use‑many‑times” principle** (single collection point, shared data flows) | Seeks to reuse the data already submitted under the existing EU‑level frameworks (AIFMD, UCITS, MMFR) and the statistical reports sent to the **ECB**, rather than creating new, separate submissions. |
| 5 | **Lay the groundwork for future technical standards** (templates, formats, frequencies) | Will feed into the upcoming **ITS/RTS** that will codify the exact data fields, reporting frequency (e.g., monthly baseline) and format (ISO 20022‑XML, XBRL or JSON) for the integrated system. |
| 6 | **Reduce the reporting burden for fund managers** while preserving supervisory relevance | By streamlining the above elements, the paper aims to keep the data that supervisors need (risk, liquidity, leverage, NAV, etc.) but eliminate unnecessary or duplicated fields. |

### How the objectives tie into existing EU reporting requirements  

- **AIFMD & UCITS** – The paper’s “stock‑take” and proposed integrated template are built on the current Article 24 (AIFMD) and Article 20a (UCITS) reporting obligations.  
- **MMFR** – The security‑by‑security granularity discussed for MMFs is meant to complement, not replace, the MMFR template.  
- **Statistical reporting (ECB)** – The integrated approach will align the supervisory data with the monthly ECB statistical regime (Regulation 2024/1988).  
- **Cross‑sectoral regimes (EMIR, SFTR, MiFID/MiFIR)** – By harmonising identifiers and semantics, the paper enables data reuse from these regimes, reducing the need for separate fund‑level submissions.  

In short, the discussion paper’s goals are to **map, harmonise, and streamline** the myriad reporting obligations that already exist across EU law, so that a single, high‑quality data set can satisfy both supervisory and statistical needs.
What are the primary objectives of ESMA’s discussion paper on the integrated collection of funds’ data and how do they relate to existing EU reporting requirements?

### Primary objectives of the discussion paper  

- **Assess the current reporting landscape** – map all supervisory and statistical reporting obligations that fund managers face under the AIFMD, UCITS, MMFR and the ECB statistical regimes.  
- **Identify and reduce duplication and inconsistencies** – highlight overlaps, gaps and semantic mismatches between the various EU‑level and national templates.  
- **Improve data standardisation and reuse** – propose a common data dictionary, harmonised identifiers (e.g. LEI, ISIN, CFI) and “report‑once” mechanisms so that the same data can satisfy several authorities.  
- **Lay the groundwork for future technical standards** – define what information must be reported, at which level of detail, in which format and with what validation rules, so that future ITS can be drafted consistently.  
- **Cut reporting burden for the industry** – by focusing on high‑added‑value elements, encouraging proportionality, and exploring integrated reporting flows (single national or EU‑wide collection points).  

### How the objectives tie to existing EU reporting requirements  

- The paper is triggered by **Directive (EU) 2024/927**, which amends the AIFMD and UCITS Directives and mandates ESMA (under Art. 69a AIFMD and Art. 20b UCITS) to report on an integrated data‑collection system by 16 April 2026.  
- It builds directly on the **current EU‑level supervisory frameworks** (AIFMD, MMFR, the revised UCITS reporting regime) and the **statistical frameworks** used by the ECB, seeking to align them rather than replace them.  
- All proposed measures (common dictionary, “report‑once”, harmonised formats such as ISO 20022 XML) are intended to work **within** the legal mandates of those directives and regulations, enhancing their effectiveness while reducing the administrative load on fund managers.  

In short, the paper’s goals are to streamline, harmonise and future‑proof the existing EU reporting architecture for funds, in line with the new legislative mandate.
Which stakeholder groups are specifically targeted by the paper, and what roles do they play in the supervisory and statistical reporting process?

The paper is aimed squarely at the players that actually **produce and file the data** required under the various fund‑reporting rules.  Its target audience is made up of:

* **Alternative Investment Fund Managers (AIFMs)** – the managers of AIFs who have to send the AIFMD supervisory templates (and related statistical data) to their home‑state competent authority and, where required, to the ECB’s statistical system.  
* **UCITS management companies and UCITS investment companies** – the entities that run UCITS funds and are responsible for the UCITS‑specific supervisory returns and the statistical filings that feed into the ECB’s fund‑statistics.  
* **Branches of EU and non‑EU fund managers** – any overseas or subsidiary units that fall under the same reporting obligations and must forward the required information to the appropriate national or EU‑level authority.  
* **Industry associations and any other organisations that take part in the reporting chain or are affected by the outcomes** – they help shape the discussion, provide feedback on the proposals and may represent the interests of the reporting entities.

In short, these groups are the **data providers** for both supervisory reporting (to national competent authorities, ESMA, the ESRB, etc.) and statistical reporting (to national central banks and the ECB). Their feedback will inform how the reporting framework can be streamlined and integrated.
Which stakeholder groups are specifically targeted by the paper, and what roles do they play in the supervisory and statistical reporting process?

### Stakeholder groups targeted by the discussion paper  

| Stakeholder group | Why they are targeted | Typical role in the **supervisory reporting** process | Typical role in the **statistical reporting** process |
|-------------------|----------------------|--------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|
| **Alternative Investment Fund Managers (AIFMs)** | Directly subject to the AIFMD reporting obligations (Article 69a). | Submit periodic and ad‑hoc data on each AIF they manage (e.g., portfolio composition, leverage, risk exposures) to their home‑Member State NCA, which may forward the data to ESMA and/or the ECB. | Provide the granular fund‑level data that feed the ECB’s investment‑fund statistical framework (security‑by‑security holdings, valuations, etc.). |
| **UCITS management companies** | Required to report under the revised UCITS reporting framework (Article 20b). | Send regular supervisory reports (annual, semi‑annual, and periodic templates) on the UCITS they manage to the home‑Member State NCA. | Supply the same data to national central banks (NCBs) for ECB statistical collection (e.g., assets, liabilities, NAV, investor breakdowns). |
| **UCITS investment companies** (i.e. funds without a designated manager) | Also fall under UCITS reporting rules. | Deliver the same supervisory information directly to the NCA. | Contribute to the statistical datasets compiled by NCBs/ECB. |
| **Branches of EU and non‑EU fund managers** | May be required to report under delegated or home‑host arrangements. | Report to the NCA of the EU home Member State (or to the host NCA where applicable) for both supervisory and statistical purposes. | Their data are used in the ECB’s euro‑area statistical aggregates. |
| **Industry associations** | Represent the interests of the above reporting entities. | Participate in the consultation, provide feedback on the design of the integrated reporting system. | Help shape the data standards that will affect statistical reporting outcomes. |
| **Any other entities involved in the reporting process or affected by its outcomes** | Includes service providers, data‑repository operators, and other market participants that handle or use the reported data. | May assist fund managers in preparing and submitting reports; may also receive processed data for oversight. | Use the aggregated or granular statistical data for analysis, research, or downstream regulatory processes. |

**In short:** the paper is aimed at the entities that actually **produce** the supervisory and statistical data (AIFMs, UCITS managers, fund branches) and at the bodies that **represent** or **support** them (industry associations, other reporting‑related entities). Their role is to collect, validate, and transmit fund‑level information that underpins both supervisory oversight (risk monitoring, compliance) and statistical analysis (monetary‑policy, macro‑prudential monitoring).
Which stakeholder groups are specifically targeted by the paper, and what roles do they play in the supervisory and statistical reporting process?

### Stakeholder groups targeted by the paper  

| Stakeholder | Typical role in the supervisory / statistical reporting chain |
|-------------|---------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Alternative Investment Fund Managers (AIFMs)** | Submit the AIFMD periodic, ad‑hoc and notification reports (e.g., portfolio composition, leverage, risk metrics) to their home‑state NCA, which forwards the data to ESMA and, where relevant, to the ECB/NCBs for statistical use. |
| **UCITS management companies** | Provide the UCITS‑specific supervisory reports (fund‑level and share‑class data, liquidity‑management information, KID disclosures) to the NCA of the home Member State; the NCA may then share the information with ESMA and the ECB for statistical aggregation. |
| **UCITS investment companies** | As the legal owners of the funds, they are required to file the same supervisory data as management companies (e.g., annual/half‑yearly financial statements, KID) and to make the information available for statistical collection. |
| **Branches of EU and non‑EU fund managers** | When operating in the EU they must comply with the EU‑level reporting templates (AIFMD, UCITS, MMFR) and send the required data to the relevant national authority, enabling both supervisory oversight and statistical reporting. |
| **Industry associations** | Act as a collective voice for their members, reviewing the draft proposals, providing feedback on practical implementation issues and suggesting alternatives to reduce reporting burden. |
| **Other entities involved in the reporting process** (e.g., custodians, depositaries, data‑service providers) | May supply reference data (LEI, ISIN, CFI) or support the validation and transmission of reports, thereby facilitating both supervisory monitoring and the ECB’s statistical data collection. |

In short, the paper is aimed at the **reporting entities** (AIFMs, UCITS managers and their branches) that generate and submit the data, and at **representative bodies** (industry associations and other ancillary participants) that help shape and support the reporting ecosystem.
How does the paper propose to harmonise supervisory reporting frameworks at the EU level, and what are the expected benefits and challenges of such harmonisation?

The paper says EU‑wide harmonisation should come from **one set of common building blocks** rather than a patchwork of national templates.  It proposes a new EU‑level reporting template that pulls together the data already required under AIFMD, UCITS and the MMF regime, backed by a **common data dictionary** and **standard identifiers** (LEI, ISIN, CFI, etc.).  By using the same **ISO 20022‑compatible XML (or XBRL/JSON) format** and a “report‑once‑use‑many‑times” approach, the same submission can be shared with ESMA, the ECB, the ESRB and the national competent authorities.  The paper also sketches three integration options – from a modest “multiple obligations with data reuse” to a full, single EU‑wide reporting hub – and suggests a modular design so that only the core data are mandatory while fund‑type‑specific modules can be added.

**Expected benefits** are a clear reduction in duplicate filings, lower compliance costs for managers, higher‑quality, comparable data and faster, more proportionate supervision across borders.  Authorities would be able to reuse data for risk monitoring, macro‑prudential analysis and statistical reporting.

**Challenges** include the current fragmentation of national frameworks (different geographic scopes, frequencies and legacy systems), semantic gaps between definitions, the need for extensive coordination among NCAs, NCBs, ESMA and the ECB, and the technical work required to align formats, validation rules and master‑data repositories.  There is also a risk that adding national‑specific extensions could re‑introduce the very duplication the harmonisation seeks to avoid.
How does the paper propose to harmonise supervisory reporting frameworks at the EU level, and what are the expected benefits and challenges of such harmonisation?

### How the paper proposes EU‑level harmonisation  

| What the paper suggests | How it would work |
|--------------------------|-------------------|
| **A single EU‑wide reporting template** (Option IR1) | A unified AIFMD/UCITS template that gathers all data points not already covered by the MMFR or ECB statistical frameworks. The template would rely on common identifiers (LEI, ISIN) and a **common data dictionary** to ensure consistent definitions. |
| **Modular “core‑plus‑add‑on” design** (Option IR2/IR3) | A core module (fund ID, main characteristics, risk metrics) is mandatory for all funds; additional modules are activated only for specific fund types (e.g., ELTIFs, MMFs) or national‑specific needs. |
| **Standardised reporting formats** | Adopt ISO 20022 XML (or XBRL/JSON where appropriate) across all regimes, with SDMX for aggregated statistical data. |
| **Centralised validation** | ESMA would apply a single set of validation rules (currently >180 rules) to all submissions, reducing duplicated checks by national authorities. |
| **Common data semantics** | Creation of an EU‑wide data dictionary (see Annex 11) to align definitions, taxonomies (CFI, ESA‑2010) and code‑lists. |

### Expected benefits  

- **Reduced duplication** – fund managers submit data once, which can be reused by NCAs, the ECB, ESRB, etc.  
- **Lower reporting burden** – fewer templates, unified frequency and formats.  
- **Higher data quality & comparability** – common identifiers and a single validation engine minimise errors.  
- **Better supervisory insight** – integrated, security‑by‑security data enable cross‑border risk analysis and macro‑prudential monitoring.  

### Anticipated challenges  

- **Geographical coverage** – EU‑wide template must accommodate euro‑area statistical requirements and non‑euro‑area funds.  
- **Frequency mismatches** – harmonising reporting cycles (monthly vs. quarterly) may clash with existing regime deadlines.  
- **Technical integration** – legacy national systems, differing XML/XBRL/JSON schemas, and the need for interoperable exchange platforms.  
- **Semantic gaps** – divergent definitions (e.g., NAV, fund classification) require extensive alignment work.  
- **National flexibility** – preserving the ability of NCAs to impose additional, proportionate data requests without re‑fragmenting the system.  

In short, the paper envisions a unified, modular reporting architecture underpinned by common data standards and a single validation process, delivering efficiency and richer data for supervisors, while recognising the need to manage jurisdictional, technical and semantic complexities.
How does the paper propose to harmonise supervisory reporting frameworks at the EU level, and what are the expected benefits and challenges of such harmonisation?

### How the paper proposes EU‑level harmonisation  

1. **Create a single EU‑wide supervisory template** – a new harmonised reporting framework that replaces the fragmented national templates for AIFMD, UCITS and MMFR (see Section 4.1 and Option IR1).  
2. **Adopt a common data dictionary** – a unified taxonomy (definitions, codes, validation rules) that all regulators will use (Section 5.2.1).  
3. **Standardise identifiers and formats** – mandatory use of LEIs, ISINs (and, where needed, CFI or ESA‑2010 codes) and a single reporting syntax (ISO 20022 XML is the preferred choice, with alternatives such as XBRL or JSON considered) (Section 5.4).  
4. **Align reporting frequencies** – the baseline is monthly reporting for all regimes, with proportionality mechanisms for smaller funds (Section 5.6).  
5. **Introduce a “report‑once, use‑many‑times” architecture** – data submitted to one collecting authority (or directly to a central ESMA hub) can be shared with other competent authorities, the ECB, ESRB, etc. (Option IR1/IR2/IR3 in Section 4.4).  

### Expected benefits  

- **Reduced duplication** – fund managers file a single dataset instead of 100+ templates, cutting compliance costs.  
- **Lower administrative burden** – fewer submissions, streamlined validation, and a proportional reporting approach.  
- **Improved data quality and comparability** – common definitions, identifiers and formats make aggregation and cross‑border supervision easier.  
- **Better supervisory insight** – richer, granular (security‑by‑security) data can be reused for risk monitoring, macro‑prudential analysis and statistical purposes.  
- **Faster, more consistent regulatory action** – uniform validation rules and a centralised repository speed up data checks and enable quicker risk detection.  

### Main challenges  

- **Geographical coverage differences** – ECB statistical reporting applies only to the euro area, while AIFMD/UCITS are Union‑wide, requiring extensions or mapping.  
- **Frequency mismatches** – current quarterly/annual regimes clash with the proposed monthly baseline, especially for MMFR.  
- **Technological integration** – legacy systems, varied data formats, and the need for interoperable exchange platforms (XML, XBRL, JSON) pose significant IT effort.  
- **National sovereignty and flexibility** – Member States may retain specific national data points or ad‑hoc requests, risking re‑fragmentation (see Option IR3).  
- **Semantic gaps** – differing definitions (e.g., NAV, fund classification) must be reconciled through the data dictionary, otherwise inconsistencies persist.  

In short, the paper envisions a unified EU supervisory reporting set‑up built on a common template, data dictionary and identifiers, delivering efficiency and better data, but it must overcome coverage, frequency, technical and national‑level hurdles.
What are the key deadlines and procedural steps outlined for submitting comments, including confidentiality and data‑protection considerations?

**Key deadlines and steps for commenting**

ESMA will take into account any feedback that it receives **by 21 September 2025**. All comments must be lodged **online through the consultation platform** that hosts this discussion paper.

When you submit your input you should:

1. **Address the specific question(s)** you are answering.  
2. **Identify the question number** so ESMA can match your response to the right issue.  
3. **Provide a clear rationale** for your view.  
4. **Suggest any alternatives** you think ESMA should consider.

**Confidentiality**  
If part of your contribution should not be made public, you need to **state that clearly and prominently in your submission**. A generic “confidentiality” line in an email is *not* sufficient. You can also request a confidential response under ESMA’s rules on access to documents; ESMA may then consult you and any decision to keep the material private can be appealed to the ESMA Board of Appeal or the European Ombudsman.

**Data‑protection**  
Details on how personal data are handled are available on ESMA’s website under the **“Legal Notice and Data protection”** section (www.esma.europa.eu).  

In short: submit your online comment by 21 Sept 2025, follow the four‑point guidance, flag any non‑public parts explicitly, and refer to ESMA’s data‑protection page for privacy information.

In what ways might the integrated data collection impact the reporting obligations of alternative investment fund managers (AIFMs) compared to UCITS management companies?

The integrated data‑collection initiative is aimed at pulling together the separate reporting streams that AIFMs and UCITS management companies currently have to deal with.  

For **AIFMs**, most of their supervisory data already flow through an EU‑wide AIFMD template, but many of them still face extra national requests (e.g., additional asset‑type breakdowns or ad‑hoc filings). An integrated system would let AIFMs **reuse the same dataset** for the AIFMD, the MMFR and the ECB statistical regime, cutting down the number of separate submissions they have to prepare. At the same time, the push for **security‑by‑security granularity** and a **common data dictionary** could mean AIFMs need to capture more detailed instrument‑level information than they do today.  

For **UCITS management companies**, reporting is currently fragmented across Member States, with each regulator demanding its own format and frequency. Integration would replace those national templates with a **single EU‑level report** (the same one used by AIFMs), standardise identifiers (LEI, ISIN, etc.) and harmonise definitions. This would dramatically **reduce duplicate filings** for UCITS firms, but they would also have to align with the richer data set that AIFMs are moving toward.  

In short, both groups would see fewer separate reports, but AIFMs may encounter a modest rise in detail requirements, while UCITS companies would benefit most from a streamlined, unified reporting process.
In what ways might the integrated data collection impact the reporting obligations of alternative investment fund managers (AIFMs) compared to UCITS management companies?

### How integrated data collection could change reporting for AIFMs vs. UCITS managers  

| Aspect | Current situation | Likely impact of an integrated collection |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| **Reporting template** | AIFMs use the AIFMD Annex IV template; UCITS managers use a separate UCITS‑level template (often national‑specific). | A single EU‑wide template would replace both (Option IR1). AIFMs and UCITS firms would submit the same data set, eliminating duplicate filings. |
| **Data semantics** | Different definitions for terms such as NAV, fund type, asset classification and AuM create inconsistencies. | A common data dictionary (Section 5.2) would standardise definitions, so AIFMs and UCITS managers would use identical field meanings and coding. |
| **Granularity** | AIFMD reporting is largely aggregated (≈2 % security‑by‑security); UCITS statistical reporting already works at security‑by‑security level. | Integrated reporting is expected to move toward **security‑by‑security** for all funds (Option SS1/SS2). AIFMs would need to provide the same detailed holdings data that UCITS managers already provide for statistical purposes. |
| **Reporting frequency** | AIFMD baseline is quarterly; UCITS reporting frequency varies nationally (often monthly). | A harmonised baseline (e.g., monthly) is being considered (Section 5.6). AIFMs would likely have to submit more frequent updates, aligning with UCITS practice. |
| **Use of master data / identifiers** | AIFMD uses LEI, ISIN, etc., but coverage is uneven; UCITS reporting already leans heavily on these identifiers. | Integrated reporting would enforce broader LEI/CFI/ISIN usage (Section 5.5), reducing ad‑hoc identifier mapping for AIFMs. |
| **Data reuse across regimes** | AIFMs currently report collateral, counterparties, and derivatives separately under EMIR, SFTR, MiFID, leading to duplication. | Integrated collection would allow **“report‑once, use‑many‑times”** (Section 4.1, Option IR1), so AIFMs could reuse the same data for supervisory and statistical purposes, cutting redundant submissions. |

**Bottom line:**  
An integrated reporting framework would align AIFMs’ obligations with those of UCITS management companies by giving them a single, harmonised template, common definitions, finer‑grained security‑by‑security data, a unified reporting frequency, and mandatory use of standard identifiers. This should reduce duplication, improve data quality, and lower the overall reporting burden for both types of managers.
In what ways might the integrated data collection impact the reporting obligations of alternative investment fund managers (AIFMs) compared to UCITS management companies?

The integrated data‑collection framework is designed to bring the AIFMD and UCITS reporting regimes onto a single “report‑once” platform.  

* **Template harmonisation** – AIFMs will keep the detailed data they already provide under AIFMD, but the new EU‑wide template (Option IR1) will also cover the information UCITS managers currently submit on a national basis. This means AIFMs will no longer need separate AIFMD and national‑level reports, while UCITS firms will have to adopt the same harmonised template instead of a patchwork of national forms.  

* **Common identifiers & data dictionary** – Both AIFMs and UCITS managers will be required to use the same identifiers (LEI, ISIN, etc.) and the same semantic definitions, reducing the need for duplicate mapping and validation work.  

* **Reporting flows** – Data will be submitted once to a single collecting authority (or directly to an EU‑level hub) and then shared with the relevant supervisors, cutting the multiple submissions that AIFMs and UCITS firms currently make to NCAs, NCBs and other regulators.  

* **Frequency and granularity** – The integration proposes a baseline monthly frequency and security‑by‑security granularity for both regimes, so AIFMs will see a modest shift in timing, while UCITS managers will move to a higher‑detail, more frequent reporting regime than they currently have.  

Overall, the impact is a reduction in duplication and administrative burden for AIFMs, and a convergence of UCITS reporting to the same level of detail, frequency and standardisation.
What alternative approaches or recommendations does the paper invite stakeholders to consider, and how could these shape future regulatory developments?

### What alternatives does the paper ask stakeholders to weigh?

The discussion paper lays out a menu of **different ways to integrate fund‑manager reporting** and invites you to tell ESMA which ‑ or which mix ‑ makes the most sense.  

* **Integrated‑reporting options** – IR1 (reuse data across existing EU‑level templates), IR2 (a single EU‑wide template that replaces all current supervisory and statistical forms) and IR3 (the IR2 model plus a limited layer for national‑specific fields).  

* **Reporting‑flow designs** – Option 1 (single national collection point that then redistributes data), Option 2 (single national collector that forwards everything to a central EU system), and Option 3 (a fully centralised EU portal where fund managers submit once and all authorities access the data).  

* **Data‑format choices** – the paper asks whether a common ISO 20022‑based XML, XBRL or JSON standard should become the norm.  

* **Granularity approaches** – full security‑by‑security reporting versus a partial, proportionate scheme that aggregates only low‑value positions, plus how share‑class data should be captured.  

* **Frequency and “report‑once” principles** – whether monthly reporting should become the baseline across all regimes, or if a hybrid of quarterly/annual with ad‑hoc bursts is preferable.  

### How could these shape future rules?

If stakeholders rally around a **single EU‑wide template and a central reporting hub**, ESMA could draft technical standards that eliminate duplicate submissions, harmonise identifiers (LEI, ISIN, CFI) and embed a common data dictionary.  
Choosing a **unified format (e.g., ISO 20022 XML)** would steer future legislative amendments toward that syntax, simplifying validation and interoperability with EMIR, SFTR and MiFID.  
A **proportionate granularity model** would let regulators keep the most useful, high‑value data while sparing smaller managers from costly security‑by‑security reporting, influencing the scope of future delegations under Article 69a of AIFMD and Article 20b of the UCITS Directive.  

In short, the alternatives you endorse now will directly feed into the technical standards and possible legislative tweaks ESMA must submit to the Commission by April 2026, shaping a more streamlined, data‑rich supervisory ecosystem.

**IR2 – Full integration** – a single EU‑wide template replaces all current supervisory and statistical templates.
**IR3 – Full integration + national extensions** – a core EU template with optional national‑specific modules. | • IR1 would let ESMA improve data sharing without immediate legislative overhaul.
• IR2 would require a new, harmonised legal framework (potentially a delegated act) but would give the greatest “report‑once” benefit.
• IR3 tries to balance EU harmonisation with Member‑State flexibility, but risks re‑introducing fragmentation if national add‑ons proliferate. | | **Reporting‑flow architecture** | **Option 1** – single national collection point (NCA or NCB) that forwards data to all other authorities.
